title: NVIDIA AI Blueprints – Quick AI 3D Renders in Blender with ComfyUI
description:
Learn how to combine Blender, ComfyUI, and the NVIDIA 3D Guided Gen AI Blueprint to generate high-quality 2D images from your 3D scenes—without traditional rendering. This step-by-step tutorial shows you how to set up NVIDIA’s AI Blueprints using an RTX 4090 GPU, configure ComfyUI inside Blender, and produce stunning AI-enhanced renders from custom 3D compositions.

Whether you're a designer, 3D artist, or tech-savvy creator, this guide simplifies the complex setup into clear, repeatable steps. No coding background required.

🔧 In This Video:
- What are NVIDIA AI Blueprints?
- Hardware requirements (RTX 4080+, 48GB+ RAM)
- Enabling virtualization in BIOS
- Installing necessary tools (Git, WSL, Microsoft C++, Blender)
- Setting up the NVIDIA NIM installer
- Hugging Face token integration
- Accepting Flux model licenses
- Cloning and running the 3D Guided Blueprint
- Blender-ComfyUI integration walkthrough
- Prompt customization and rendering settings
- Saving outputs and generating vari
transcript: Welcome to another AI tutorial video.  Today's video is not part of the weekly episode  because it uses comfy UI in a different way.  We will be combining Blender with comfy UI.  So today, we will discuss Nvidia AI Blueprints,  which are pre-designed customizable workflows  that help developers quickly create generative AI applications.  They provide sample code, documentation,  and integration with Nvidia tools like NIM, microservices,  making it easy to build complex AI systems.  Think of it like having a set of detailed Blueprints  to construct a building, and everything  you need to get started is already laid out for you.  I am focusing on one of those blueprints  called the 3D Guided Generative AI Blueprint.  This specialized blueprint is designed specifically  for RTX AI PCs.  It allows creators to generate high-quality 2D images  by first building a 3D scene in Blender,  which serves as a guide for the AI model  to produce a realistic 2D output.  It's like constructing a 3D model  and then generating realistic images from any angle,  giving you much more control and precision  than just using text-based prompts.  On GitHub, they have an Nvidia AI Blueprints page  with different examples of how NIM can be leveraged  to build innovative solutions.  There's a list of repositories specialized  for various purposes, but since I'm a graphic designer,  I'll focus on image generation,  so today I'll present the one called 3D Guided Gen AI RTX.  On their page, you have pretty much everything  you need to get started, but there are a lot of steps  which can be a bit intimidating,  especially for someone like me who isn't a programmer.  It may seem like there are many installations,  but it ran without a problem for me.  In this video, I will show you all the steps  I followed to make it work.  Now, this is very important.  It only works on a few RTX graphics cards.  Here's the list of graphics cards  that can run this blueprint.  It's compatible with RTX 4080 or higher.  They also recommend at least 48 gigabytes of RAM.  So if you don't meet these requirements,  you probably won't be able to run these blueprints.  I'll be testing it on an RTX 4090 graphics card  since that's the one I have.  Let's start with the first step.  You need to enable virtualization for this to work  since every motherboard BIOS is different.  Search online for how to enable virtualization  for your specific motherboard model.  For example, I have a Z790 series motherboard.  Here's how I enabled it.  I restarted the PC and pressed delete or F2  to enter the BIOS.  Then I switched to advanced mode,  clicked on tweaker and selected the advanced CPU settings.  I found Intel VMX virtualization technology  and set it to enabled.  After that, I saved and exited using the appropriate button.  The PC restarted and we can continue with a step two  as shown on the GitHub page, which is the prerequisites.  The NIM prerequisite installer requires user account control  to be enabled, but it should be enabled by default  unless you disabled it yourself.  So a step two, we download this installer  and save it somewhere on your hard drive.  Then go to where you saved it and extract the contents  of the archive.  This will give you the setup file, double-click to open  the installer and as you can see, it asks you to confirm  that you have enabled virtualization. The installation will begin and once it finishes,  you should see a message saying successfully installed. Moving to step five, where we need to install Blender,  again, we can copy the command for installing the Blender  version that was tested with this blueprint. If you already have an account, simply log in,  add your username and password,  and this is how it looks when you are logged in.  Next, go to the settings and click on access tokens. Now we can run the scene by pressing the run button.  You'll see that it takes the current scene  and together with the prompt, it will generate a new image.
